title: Premium Subscription Ladder Dispute Pattern with Sequential Digital Goods
narrative: Cardholder initiates legitimate free trial on streaming platform or SaaS service, upgrades through 2-3 tier levels over 14-21 days (e.g., $9.99â†’$24.99â†’$49.99/month), then disputes all charges as unauthorized after 45-90 days of usage. Pattern observed with ProductCD='W' (digital services), featuring deliberate account engagementâ€”password changes within first 7 days, preference modifications, content downloads exceeding 500MB. Disputes filed exactly 1-3 days before next billing cycle to maximize free usage window. Typical sequence shows card1_txn_count_7d=1 during trial, escalating to card1_txn_count_30d=3-4 as tiers increase. Email domains cluster in free providers (P_emaildomain in gmail.com, yahoo.com) with addr1 matching billing address exactly. Chargeback reason code 83 (fraud-card not present) despite CVV match on all transactions and consistent device fingerprint.
indicators: ProductCD='W' with 2-4 sequential subscription tier upgrades within 21-day window, amounts increasing 150-400%
card1_txn_count_30d shows exactly 3-4 transactions all to same MID, zero transactions to other merchants
Dispute filed 45-90 days post-initial transaction, 1-3 days before next scheduled billing cycle
All transactions show CVV_match=1, AVS_match='Y', dist1=0 (billing=shipping), consistent device_id
Account activity logs show 15+ logins, 3+ preference changes, content consumption >500MB before dispute
P_emaildomain in top-10 free providers, account created within 24 hours of first transaction
card1_amt_zscore remains <1.5 across all transactions, suggesting amounts align with cardholder's normal spending pattern
summary: A customer legitimately signs up for a service trial, systematically upgrades through multiple subscription tiers over 2-3 weeks while actively using the account, then disputes all charges as unauthorized 45-90 days later—typically just before the next billing cycle.
reasoning: The cardholder intentionally uses the service extensively with password changes, downloads, and preference modifications, then falsely claims fraud to avoid payment after maximizing their usage period, exploiting chargeback protections despite all security checks passing.
indicator_explanations: Customer systematically upgraded through premium tiers then disputed the entire sequence
Card exclusively used at this merchant with no other purchase activity
Dispute timing strategically placed to avoid next billing while maximizing service window
All fraud checks passed perfectly suggesting legitimate cardholder making authorized purchases
Heavy platform engagement proves customer actively used services before claiming fraud
Free email registered same day as purchase suggests potential throwaway dispute setup
Transaction amounts fit cardholder's normal spending making chargeback claim questionable
